explanation: The verb entails a transition into a new state, and the focus is on that transition rather than on a situation holding over time.

FullText: » He received the kingdom and went back to check on his servants . He wanted to know what they had gained by trading with the money he gave them .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (gaining something by trading) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: » The first reported : Your pound has made ten pounds more .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (making ten pounds more) rather than a situation holding continuously across the time span.

FullText: » The second said : Your pound , Lord , has made five pounds .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event of making five pounds, rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, thus it is not DtUniv.

FullText: He told them , » Go into the nearby village . You will find a colt tied . No man has ever sat on him . Untie him and bring him to me .
explanation: The clause describes a single event (sitting) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous state or habitual series.

FullText: He was near the descent of the Mount of Olives . The entire crowd of disciples rejoiced and praised God with a loud voice for all the mighty works they had seen .
explanation: The clause describes discrete events (mighty works) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: He said : » It is written my house shall be a house of prayer . You have made it a den of robbers . «
Target: have made it (a den of robbers)
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (making the house a den of robbers) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: Some of the scribes answered , » Teacher you have said well . «
explanation: The clause describes a single instance of saying something, not a continuous state or activity.

FullText: He said to them : » I have earnestly wanted to eat this Passover with you before I die .
explanation: The clause describes a desire leading up to a specific event (dying), not a continuous state or activity over a time span.

FullText: He received a cup , and when he had given thanks , he said : » Take this , and share it among yourselves .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (giving thanks) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: » The Son of man indeed goes , as it has been determined . But woe to that man through whom he is betrayed ! «
explanation: The clause describes a single event (being determined) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: » You have continued with me in my temptations .
explanation: The predicate 'have continued' denotes a non-terminating situation that holds throughout the entire time span, indicating a continuous state from a past starting point up to the reference time.

FullText: » I prayed for you that your faith would not fail . Once you have turned again strengthen your brothers . «
explanation: The verb entails a transition into a new state, and the focus is on that transition rather than on a situation holding over time.

FullText: They responded : » What further need do we have for witnesses ? We have heard this from his mouth . «
explanation: The clause describes a completed action of hearing, not a continuous state or activity.

FullText: Herod was very happy to see Jesus . He had wanted to see him for a long time . He hoped to see him perform a miracle .
explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time' indicating a situation that extends to the reference time, and the predicate 'wanted' denotes a non-terminating situation that holds throughout the entire time span.

FullText: He said : » You brought this man to me as one who perverted the people . I examined him before you and find no fault in this man . He has not done the things you accuse him of .
Target: has not done (the things you accuse him of)
explanation: The clause describes a lack of discrete events ('the things you accuse him of') rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: » Herod found no fault and sent him back to us . He has done nothing worthy of death .
explanation: The clause describes a state of inaction, but not one that necessarily holds continuously across a time span; it is more of a summary statement about past actions.

FullText: A third time he said to them : » Why , what evil has this man done ? I have found no reason to put him to death . I will therefore chastise him and release him . «
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event ('done evil') rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: A third time he said to them : » Why , what evil has this man done ? I have found no reason to put him to death . I will therefore chastise him and release him . «
explanation: The clause describes a state resulting from a past event ('found no reason'), rather than a situation holding continuously over time.

FullText: » We are indeed justly condemned for we receive the due reward of our deeds . But this man has done nothing wrong . «
explanation: The clause describes a state of not doing wrong, but it does not contain duration adverbials or indicate a situation that extends to the reference time, nor does it describe a repeated or habitual series of events.

FullText: It was early in the morning on the first day of the week . They went to the tomb with the spices that they had prepared .
explanation: The clause describes a completed action of preparing spices, rather than a continuous state or activity holding over a time span.

FullText: They were perplexed about what had happened . Two men stood by them in dazzling apparel .

FullText: » He is not here . He has arisen ! Remember how he spoke to you when he was yet in Galilee .

explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (seeing a vision) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: He said to them : » You are foolish men . You are slow of heart to believe in all that the prophets have spoken !
explanation: The clause describes discrete events ('spoken') rather than a situation holding continuously across the time span, and there are no duration adverbials.

FullText: When he had sat down with them to eat he took the bread and blessed ; and breaking it he gave to them .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (sitting down) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: » The Lord has risen indeed , « they said , » and he appeared to Simon . «
explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a transition into a new state, and the focus is on that transition rather than on a situation holding over time.

explanation: The clause describes a state ('seen') that has held continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time, indicating a universal duration.

FullText: » I have seen , and have borne witness that this is the Son of God . «
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event ('seen') rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

explanation: The clause describes a discrete event of bearing witness, not a continuous situation holding across a time span.

explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (finding the Messiah) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

explanation: The clause describes a discrete event (finding someone) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «

FullText: » This is the truth . We speak what we know and witness about what we have seen . But you do not receive our testimony .
explanation: The clause describes a discrete event ('seen') rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.

explanation: The clause describes a single, bounded event (ascension) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, thus it is not DtUniv.

FullText: » He who has an active faith in him is not judged . He who does not have an active faith has been judged already for he has not had faith in the name of the only begotten Son of God .
explanation: The clause describes a state ('has been judged') that has held continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time, implying a continuous state of being judged.

FullText: » He who has an active faith in him is not judged . He who does not have an active faith has been judged already for he has not had faith in the name of the only begotten Son of God .
explanation: The clause describes a state of not having faith, but there is no duration adverbial or other indication that this state holds continuously over a time span; it is more of a punctual result of not having faith at a specific time.

FullText: » This is the judgment , that the light has come into the world . People loved the darkness rather than the light for their works were evil .
explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a transition into a new state, and the focus is on that transition rather than on a situation holding over time.

explanation: The clause describes discrete events of seeing and hearing, rather than a continuous situation holding across a time span.

FullText: » For you have had five husbands . The one you have now is not your husband , this is true . «

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .

FullText: Many in the crowd believed in him . They asked : » When the Christ comes will he do more signs than those this man has done ? «

explanation: The clause describes discrete events ('seen' and 'heard') rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
